A generic envelope for API responses, providing a consistent wire format that wraps the data payload alongside success/failure status and metadata.

remarks.text: All API endpoints should return
to give clients a uniform structure for deserialization. The
flag allows clients to branch on success or failure without inspecting HTTP status codes, while
provides server-side timing for debugging and cache-invalidation purposes. For error responses,
will be
and
will contain a human-readable error description. For richer error bodies, consider returning
as the
payload.

var response = ApiResponse<ProductDto>.Fail("Product not found");
     // { "data": null, "message": "Product not found", "success": false, ... }

Specifies the preferred communication channel for contacting a customer.

remarks.text: This enumeration is used by the notification subsystem to select the appropriate delivery mechanism. It is stored as its integer value in the database and serialised as a string in JSON responses.

enumMembers.name: Email
enumMembers.description: Contact via email. This is the default channel.

enumMembers.name: Phone
enumMembers.description: Contact via a voice phone call.

enumMembers.name: Sms
enumMembers.description: Contact via short message service (SMS / text message).

summary.text: Represents a customer entity in the system, serving as the primary domain model for all customer-related operations including creation, retrieval, and segmentation.

remarks.text: A
is the aggregate root for customer data. Every customer is assigned a globally unique
at creation time and must have a non-empty
. Customers are active by default and can be deactivated through
. Tags enable flexible segmentation—for example, marking customers as "vip", "wholesale", or "beta-tester"—without requiring schema changes. The
property controls which channel is used for outbound communications.

description.text: Creating a new customer with required properties:

summary.text: Gets or sets the UTC date and time when this customer record was created.

remarks.text: Automatically set to
at construction. This value should not be modified after the initial persistence operation.

summary.text: Gets or sets the customer's email address, used for account verification and transactional notifications.

remarks.text: When
, the customer has not provided an email and cannot receive email-based communications. Validation should enforce RFC 5322 format when a value is present.

summary.text: Gets or sets the customer's full display name.

remarks.text: This property is required and must not be
or whitespace. It is used throughout the UI and in transactional emails as the primary way to address the customer.

summary.text: Gets or sets the globally unique identifier for this customer.

remarks.text: This value is typically generated by the caller at construction time using
. It serves as the primary key across all persistence layers and is immutable after creation.

summary.text: Gets or sets a value indicating whether the customer account is currently active.

remarks.text: Inactive customers are excluded from search results and cannot place new orders. Use
to set this to
in a controlled manner that also raises the appropriate domain events.

summary.text: Gets or sets the customer's preferred communication channel.

remarks.text: Defaults to
. When sending notifications, the system should honour this preference and fall back to email only when the preferred channel is unavailable.

summary.text: Gets or sets the list of free-form tags used for customer segmentation and targeted marketing campaigns.

remarks.text: Tags are case-insensitive string labels. Common conventions include lowercase kebab-case (e.g., "early-adopter", "high-value"). An empty list indicates that no segmentation has been applied.

examples.code: customer.Tags.Add("loyalty-program");

description.text: Adding a segmentation tag to an existing customer:

to allow bitwise combination of values. Use

summary.text: Defines the contract for managing customer lifecycle operations including creation, retrieval, search, update, and deactivation.

remarks.text: Implementations of

are responsible for enforcing business rules such as email uniqueness, required-field validation, and the invariant that deactivated customers cannot place new orders. All methods are asynchronous and return
to support non-blocking I/O against the underlying data store. Callers should handle
returns from
gracefully, as a missing customer is a normal condition rather than an error.

summary.text: Marks a customer as inactive, preventing them from placing new orders or appearing in search results.

remarks.text: Deactivation is a soft operation\u2014the customer record is retained in the data store with
set to
. This method is idempotent: calling it on an already-inactive customer returns
without side effects.

returns.text: if the customer was found and successfully deactivated;
if no customer with the given
exists or the customer was already inactive.

summary.text: Searches for customers whose
or
matches the given query string, with support for offset-based pagination.

returns.text: A read-only list of customers matching the search criteria, ordered by
ascending. Returns an empty list when no matches are found.

query.text: A case-insensitive search string. The implementation performs a contains-style match against

skip.text: The number of matching results to skip, enabling offset-based pagination. Defaults to 0.

take.text: The maximum number of results to return per page. Defaults to 25. Values above 100 may be clamped by the implementation.

examples.code:
var page1 = await service.SearchAsync("contoso", skip: 0, take: 10);
    var page2 = await service.SearchAsync("contoso", skip: 10, take: 10);

summary.text: A service that publishes notifications for key order lifecycle events.

remarks.text: Consumers subscribe to the events exposed by this interface to react to order state transitions. Implementations are responsible for raising events at the appropriate points in the order processing pipeline.

record struct to guarantee immutability and value-based equality. Using
instead of a bare
prevents subtle bugs where values in different currencies are summed or compared. The
is stored as a
to avoid the floating-point precision issues inherent to
in financial calculations.

Represents a single line item within an
, capturing the product, quantity, and price at the time of purchase.

remarks.text: Prices are captured at order time and do not change if the product's catalogue price is updated later. The line total can be calculated as
Quantity * UnitPrice

summary.text: Gets or sets the display name of the product being ordered.

remarks.text: This is a snapshot of the product name at order time. It is stored denormalized so that the order remains readable even if the product is renamed or removed from the catalogue.

summary.text: Gets or sets the number of units ordered.

remarks.text: Must be a positive integer. Defaults to 1.

summary.text: Gets or sets the unit price of the product at the time the order was placed.

remarks.text: Stored as a
to avoid floating-point rounding errors in financial calculations.

summary.text: Defines the lifecycle states an
progresses through from initial placement to final fulfilment or cancellation.

remarks.text: The legal transitions form a linear pipeline with an escape hatch:
. An order may be moved to
at any point before

enumMembers.name: Pending
enumMembers.description: The order has been created but has not yet been reviewed or confirmed.

enumMembers.name: Confirmed
enumMembers.description: Payment has been verified and the order is approved for fulfilment.

enumMembers.name: Shipped
enumMembers.description: The order has left the warehouse and is in transit to the customer.

enumMembers.name: Delivered
enumMembers.description: The order has been received by the customer, completing the fulfilment cycle.

enumMembers.name: Cancelled
enumMembers.description: The order was cancelled before shipment. Cancelled orders cannot be reinstated.

and other paged query methods. The

A machine-readable error response body following the RFC 9457 (Problem Details for HTTP APIs) specification, providing a standardized structure that clients can parse without knowledge of the specific API.

remarks.text: Every field in
is optional. At minimum, set
and
. Use
to point to a documentation page describing the error category. The
dictionary allows attaching arbitrary additional context—common patterns include:

summary.text: Gets or sets a human-readable explanation specific to this occurrence of the problem.
Gets the extension members for this problem details instance.

remarks.text: Use this dictionary to include additional context such as trace identifiers, validation errors, or retry-after hints.

summary.text: Gets or sets a URI reference that identifies the specific occurrence of the problem.
Gets or sets the HTTP status code generated by the origin server for this occurrence.
Gets or sets a short, human-readable summary of the problem type.
Gets or sets a URI reference that identifies the problem type.

, the product should not appear in storefront listings but may still be visible in the admin catalogue.

to preserve precision in financial calculations. Negative values are not permitted.

remarks.text: SKU codes follow the format
where XX is a two-letter category prefix and #### is a numeric sequence.

remarks.text: Tags enable faceted search and filtering. They should be lowercase, hyphen-separated strings (e.g., "usb-c", "wireless").

from the application's configuration section (e.g., "RateLimiting") to control how many requests a client may make within a sliding time window.
